title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana dashboard — that consistent memory climb pattern is really helpful.

I'd like to understand the timeline a bit better: you mentioned you've been restarting every evening for the past week. Did this slowdown start about a week ago, or had you been noticing it for longer before the restarts became necessary? And if something did change around that time — a TaskFlow version upgrade, a configuration change, a jump in usage, new integrations — what comes to mind?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question — yes, this started exactly when we upgraded from TaskFlow v2.2 to v2.3 about a week ago. Before that upgrade, the server was rock solid and we never needed to restart it. No other changes were made around that time — same number of users, same configuration, same VM. The only thing that changed was the TaskFlow version. I remember the v2.3 changelog mentioned something about 'improved real-time notifications' but I don't know if that's related.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really strong signal — a clean correlation with the v2.3 upgrade and no other changes. The real-time notifications feature you mentioned is definitely worth exploring.

Can you tell me more about how your team uses notifications in TaskFlow? For example, do your 200 users tend to keep TaskFlow open in a browser tab throughout the day, or do they pop in and out? And when you look at your server logs (or that Grafana dashboard), do you notice anything climbing alongside the memory — like the number of active connections or WebSocket connections increasing over the day without dropping back down?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
Most of our users keep TaskFlow open in a browser tab throughout the day — it's their main project management tool. They're constantly creating tasks, commenting, updating statuses, etc. I'd estimate each user is making maybe 20-30 actions per hour on average.

As for the server side, I do have a Grafana panel tracking WebSocket connections and I actually noticed something odd there. The number of WebSocket connections or event listeners seems to climb steadily throughout the day and never really drops, even when users close their tabs or go idle. It's like something is being registered but never cleaned up. By end of day the count is in the tens of thousands, which seems way too high for 200 users. I wasn't sure if that was normal for the new notification system or not, but now that you're asking, it does seem suspicious.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in v2.3 real-time notification system: WebSocket connections/event listeners never cleaned up","problem":"After upgrading from TaskFlow v2.2 to v2.3, the server's memory usage climbs steadily from ~500MB to 4GB+ over the course of a day, causing progressive slowdown (10+ second page loads, API timeouts) and requiring a daily restart. The issue affects all 200 active users by late afternoon.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The v2.3 'improved real-time notifications' feature registers WebSocket connections or server-side event listeners for each user action or session but never tears them down when users disconnect, close tabs, or go idle. This causes unbounded accumulation — tens of thousands of leaked connections/listeners per day for 200 active users — leading to memory exhaustion and degraded performance.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a server (the reporter's VM or a test instance)","Connect ~200 users (or simulate with a load tool) who keep the app open in browser tabs","Have users perform normal activity (creating tasks, commenting, updating statuses) over several hours","Monitor memory usage and WebSocket/event listener count via Grafana or server metrics","Observe that memory and connection count climb steadily and never reclaim, even as users close tabs or go idle","Compare against the same scenario on v2.2 to confirm the regression"],"environment":"Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2 one week ago). ~200 active users who keep TaskFlow open throughout the workday. Same VM, configuration, and user count as before the upgrade. Grafana monitoring in place.","severity":"high","impact":"All 200 users experience progressively degraded performance throughout the day, reaching unusable levels (10+ second loads, API timeouts) by late afternoon. Requires daily manual restart, creating a recurring outage window and operational burden.","recommended_fix":"Investigate the real-time notification subsystem introduced or modified in v2.3. Specifically: (1) Trace the WebSocket or SSE connection lifecycle — look for missing cleanup on disconnect, tab close, or idle timeout events. (2) Check for server-side event listener registration (e.g., pub/sub subscriptions, in-memory listener arrays) that are added per-connection but never removed. (3) Verify that the server handles the browser 'beforeunload' / WebSocket 'close' events and tears down all associated resources. (4) Diff the v2.3 notification code against v2.2 to identify what changed.","proposed_test_case":"Create an integration test that opens N WebSocket connections to the notification endpoint, performs some actions, then closes all connections. Assert that after a short grace period, the server's tracked connection/listener count returns to zero and memory usage does not grow proportionally with the number of historical connections. Run this in a loop to simulate a full day's connection churn.","information_gaps":["Exact server runtime (Node.js, Python, Java, etc.) and notification transport (WebSocket, SSE, long-polling) — not critical since the v2.3 diff will reveal this","Whether the leak is in connection objects, event listeners, or both","Server-side error logs that might show failed cleanup attempts"]}
